text: Continuing to exploit the theological tensions of Romans 7:7–25, the ‘helplessness of the law’ becomes a key element of the total crisis of sin that demands divine action. This study argues that the clause τὸ ... ἀδύνατον in Romans 8:3 is an accusative of respect with dependent clauses rather than being an anacoluthon as commonly supposed. Paul shows that the secondary consequences of the crisis in Romans 8 — the helplessness of the flesh and the inability of the law — are the dominant references for his statements in the period. Romans 8:3–4 then represents Paul’s complete resolution of the three aspects of the crisis in sin. There, in the Son of God, through his incarnation and mission, God acts so that sin has ceased to be the reigning power of life and so that the power of the human flesh is annihilated because sin in the flesh of the Son has been condemned. Through this Christ is able to nullify the powerlessness of the law in that by the flesh of the Son, in whom God’s law resides, the law’s just requirements are fully vindicated.
